items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.

items.q: What is the key difference between a compiler and an interpreter?
items.correct: A compiler translates the whole program first; an interpreter executes line by line.
items.misconception: A compiler is a person, an interpreter is software.
items.why: Students confuse the human meanings with technical ones. In CS, both are programs. Compilers produce an executable; interpreters run source directly each time.

items.q: What is hexadecimal A in denary?
items.why: Students forget hex uses A-F to represent 10-15 because we need 16 distinct symbols. A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15.

items.q: Who proved the halting problem undecidable?
items.why: It's Turing. “Church” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A problem that no algorithm can solve for all inputs is:
items.why: It's Undecidable. “Tractable” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Turing publish the halting-problem proof?
items.why: Exact answer: 1936. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
